editableNewDialogue.text: For the Phase Two slate, can you give me taglines for each film and one unifying theme sentence for the whole phase?

Unifying Phase Two theme: “Every victory casts a longer shadow—across cities, across myths, and across worlds.”

1) Superman: Shadow of the Unknown — “When the truth arrives from the stars, Metropolis becomes the price.”
2) The Flash & Green Lantern: Clashing Forces — “Fast meets fearless—until fear learns their names.”
3) Wonder Woman: Daughter of Zeus — “Blood of gods. Will of mortals.”
4) Batman & Nightwing: Blood Ties — “Family is the hardest case to solve.”
5) Aquaman: The Wrath of the Seven Seas — “A king without a throne still has a sea to answer to.”
6) Justice League Dark: Arcane Awakening — “Some battles can’t be won in daylight.”
7) Justice League: Infinite Crisis — “To save their world, they must face what they could become.”
Nice—now pitch a single crossover post-credits scene that could appear in two different Phase Two movies, but plays slightly differently each time.

Post-credits concept: “The Same Warning, Two Perspectives”

Core beat (shared): In a sealed, lightless chamber, an impossible “tear” in space hangs like a black mirror. A distorted transmission leaks through—voices layered on top of each other, as if multiple Earths are talking at once. The message isn’t a threat; it’s a plea: “Do not trust the symbol. It wins by wearing you.” A final flicker reveals a split-second silhouette of a hero-shaped figure—familiar, but wrong.

Version A (attached to Superman: Shadow of the Unknown): The scene is framed as alien tech fallout. The tear appears inside a Brainiac-adjacent containment vault (or a Kryptonian relic chamber), and Superman hears the warning as a corrupted signal he can *almost* understand. He focuses on the word “symbol,” and the camera lingers on his crest reflected in the black mirror—then the reflection blinks out of sync.

Version B (attached to Justice League Dark: Arcane Awakening): The scene is framed as occult fallout. The tear manifests during a ritual “aftershock,” and Constantine (or Zatanna) recognizes it as a multiversal breach with a parasitic intelligence. The warning comes through as a chant in dead languages, translated on-the-fly: “It wears the cape. It wears the crown. It wears the cowl.” The black mirror briefly shows *their* silhouettes, each one slightly twisted, as if reality is rehearsing replacements.

Both versions end the same way: someone off-screen shuts the chamber—too late. A tiny sliver of the black mirror remains, and from it comes a quiet second line, spoken in a calm, familiar voice: “I’m already here.”
Phase 2 is solid—can you keep the releases spaced like that (spring/summer/fall), and make sure each movie still feeds into the big crossover the way "Infinite Crisis" does?

Absolutely—I'll keep a consistent seasonal cadence (spring/summer/fall windows) and build clear connective tissue in each film (post-credits, recurring artifacts/antagonists, and escalating consequences) so the crossover payoff feels earned in "Infinite Crisis."

Actually, for the record, I don’t really want this to be tragic. I’d rather it be uplifting and hopeful overall. Emotional, sure—but not a downer, and it can still have a pretty classic hero-vs-villain through-line as long as the character work is strong.

Got it—so the priority is emotional and hopeful, with an uplifting resolution, and you’re fine with a more traditional hero-versus-villain structure as the backbone (as long as it stays character-driven).
Yes, exactly. I’m not chasing tragedy here—legacy can be inspiring instead.
